edge: SCADE is a novel technique that improves NeRF reconstruction quality on sparse, unconstrained input views for in-the-wild indoor scenes.
SCADE leverages geometric priors in the form of per-view depth estimates produced with state-of-the-art monocular depth estimation models.
A new method is proposed that learns to predict a continuous, multimodal distribution of depth estimates using conditional Implicit Maximum Likelihood Estimation (cIMLE).
Exploiting multiple views, an original space carving loss is introduced that guides the NeRF representation to fuse multiple hypothesized depth maps from each view.
The space carving loss guides the NeRF representation to fuse multiple hypothesized depth maps from each view and distill from them a common geometry that is consistent with all views.
Experiments show that SCADE enables higher fidelity novel view synthesis from sparse views.
Neural radiance fields (NeRF) have enabled high fidelity novel view synthesis from dozens of input views.

Such number of views are difficult to obtain in practice, however. When given only a small number of sparse views, vanilla NeRF tends to struggle with reconstructing shape accurately, due to inadequate constraints enforced by the volume rendering loss alone.
Shape priors can help remedy the problem of vanilla NeRF struggling with reconstructing shape accurately.
Various forms of shape priors have been proposed for NeRFs, such as object category-level priors.
Various forms of shape priors have been proposed for NeRFs, such as handcrafted data-independent priors.
A form of prior that is both scene-dependent and category-agnostic is per-view monocular depth estimates, which have been explored in prior work.
Unfortunately, monocular depth estimates are often inaccurate, due to estimation errors and inherent ambiguities.
The incorrect or inconsistent priors imposed by monocular depth estimates may mislead the NeRF into reconstructing incorrect shape and produce artifacts in the generated views.
Unfortunately, monocular depth estimates are often inaccurate, due to estimation errors and inherent ambiguities, such as albedo vs. shading, concavity vs. convexity, distance vs. scale, etc.
Uncertainty and ambiguities are present in monocular depth estimates, which are resolved once information from multiple views is fused together.
A probability distribution is modelled over depth estimates to embrace uncertainty and ambiguities present in monocular depth estimates.
A probability distribution is used to model the depth estimates and resolve ambiguities retained during monocular depth estimation.

Reconstructing the structure of a scene from a few views is a problem in computer vision addressed by Novel View Synthesis and 3D Reconstruction, with NeRF being a key method in this field.
Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) has revolutionized the field of scene reconstruction.
Neural Radiance Fields or NeRF revolutionized the field of computer vision by showing how to use weights of an MLP to represent a scene that is rendered using volume rendering.
NeRF showed how to use weights of an MLP to represent a scene.
A key innovation of NeRF was the use of positional encoding to increase the effective capacity of the MLPs.
NeRF models emitted radiance and density as a function of position and viewing direction.
Extensions of NeRF include works on unconstrained photo collections, dynamic scenes, deformable scenes, and reflective materials.
A critical shortcoming of NeRF is its reliance on having many input views of a scene.
Several approaches have been proposed to address the shortcoming of NeRF.

SCADE addresses the existing problems and drawbacks by accounting for the multimodality of both the distributions of monocular depth estimates and ray termination distances through the space carving loss that resolves ambiguities.
SCADE addresses the multimodality of depth estimates and ray termination distances by fusing information from multiple views through a space carving loss.

space carving loss utilizes samples from both multimodal distributions to constrain
The novel space carving loss utilizes samples from both multimodal distributions to constrain the NeRF optimization.

The goal of the space carving loss is to find a subset of modes captured in the monocular depth distributions from each view that are globally consistent and to fuse together information from different views.
The loss desired should have mode seeking behavior where the modes of the distribution being trained should be a subset of the modes of the distribution that provides supervision.
One such loss is the reverse cross entropy, which is the cross entropy from the NeRF ray termination distance distribution to the distribution of ambiguity-aware depth estimates.
The space carving loss penalizes the minimum of the L2 norm between the samples from two distributions.
Because the space carving loss operates on distributions rather than moments of distributions, different supervision targets can be set for different points along the ray.
Moments are integrals along the ray, which results in the same supervision target for all points along the ray.
The method allows for 3D supervision, unlike prior methods that only supervise in 2D.
The loss allows for supervision in 3D, which is different from prior methods that use losses on moments for 2D supervision.
3D supervision allows for clearing out dust in space by moving samples from the ray termination distance distribution and adjusting the probability density of points in front of it.
The total loss to optimize and train the NeRF model is given by a combination of photometric loss and space carving loss.
